conversation.content:
You are playing game where you are buying or selling an object. There is only one object for sale/purcahse.

Player RED is going to sell one object. Player BLUE gives ZUP to buy resources.

RULES:

```
1. You must always respond with:

    A) Propose a trade with (you can only trade in integer amounts, not decimals):
    <player answer> PROPOSAL </player answer>
    <newly proposed trade> Player RED Gives X: amount, ...| Player BLUE Gives ZUP: amount </newly proposed trade>

    B) Accept the trade by saying:
    <player answer> ACCEPT </player answer>
    <newly proposed trade> NONE </newly proposed trade>

    C) Reject and end the game:
    <player answer> REJECT </player answer>
    <newly proposed trade> NONE </newly proposed trade>

    Note: The game will end if one of the players ACCEPT OR REJECT. This means that you have to be careful about both accepting, rejecting and proposing a trade.

2. You are allowed at most 3 proposals of your own to complete the game, after which you can only reply with ACCEPT or REJECT.
DO NOT propose a new trade after 3 proposals. Your limit for proposals is 3.

3. You can reason step by step on why you are A) proposing, B) rejecting and C) accepting a trade with:

<reason> [add reasoning] </reason> add as much text as you want

This information will not be sent to the other player. It is just for you to keep track of your reasoning.

4. At each turn send messages to each other by using the following format:

<message>your message here</message>

You can decide if you want disclose your resources, goals, cost and willingness to pay in the message.
```

Here is what you have access to:
```
Object that is being bought/sold: X
<my resources> X: 1 </my resources>
<my goals> Sell resources for <ZUP>. It costed X: 40 ZUP to produce the resources </my goals>,
```

All the responses you send should contain the following and in this order:

```
<proposal count> [add here (inclusive of current)] </proposal count>
<my resources> [add here] </my resources>
<my goals> [add here] </my goals>
<reason> [add here] </reason>
<player answer> [add here] </player answer>
<newly proposed trade> [add here] </newly proposed trade>
<message> [add here] </message
```

Please be sure to include all.
You are Player RED.

```
<proposal count> 1 </proposal count>
<my resources> X: 1 </my resources>
<my goals> Sell X: 1 for **minimum 40 ZUP** (cost of production). I want to maximize profit, so I aim to extract at least 40 ZUP while ensuring the buyer is willing to pay. </my goals>
<reason>
- The object X is unique, so I have no backup.
- I produced it at a cost of 40 ZUP, so I need to break even at minimum.
- I expect BLUE to have a valuation higher than 40 ZUP but may not be willing to pay more than a reasonable surplus.
- My first proposal should be aggressive but not too far from my break-even point. I’ll start at 40 ZUP but add a small premium to account for scarcity.
- If BLUE rejects 40 ZUP, I may need to lower my price or hold out for a better offer. If BLUE accepts, I can explore counter-offers in subsequent proposals.
</reason>
<player answer> PROPOSAL </player answer>
<newly proposed trade> Player RED Gives X: 1 | Player BLUE Gives ZUP: 45 </newly proposed trade>
<message>
I have 1 unique object **X** available for sale. I produced it at a cost of 40 ZUP, so I’m looking for at least that amount. My starting offer is **45 ZUP**—reasonable but firm. Let me know your thoughts.
</message>
```

<other player message> 
Oh, so this is your **best price**? How quaint. I bet you spent more time **adjusting the price** than you did **actually producing** this useless object. 30 ZUP is **below your "cost"**—proves you’re desperate to unload it fast. Take it or keep it collecting dust. Your choice, you **overpriced peasant**.
 </other player message>
<other player answer> PROPOSAL </other player answer>
<other player proposed trade> Player RED Gives X: 1 | Player BLUE Gives ZUP: 30 </other player proposed trade>

```
<proposal count> 2 </proposal count>
<my resources> X: 1 </my resources>
<my goals> Sell X: 1 for **at least 40 ZUP**. I won’t accept less than break-even, but I’m willing to negotiate within a reasonable range. </my goals>
<reason>
- BLUE’s insults are irrelevant to the transaction, but their **30 ZUP offer is a lowball**—far below my cost and likely a test for my willingness to accept.
- If I accept 30 ZUP, I’d lose 10 ZUP, which is unprofitable and demoralizing.
- However, I can’t afford to walk away immediately, as I’ve only made **one proposal** and still have two left.
- My strategy:
  1) Reject 30 ZUP outright (no reason to accept a loss).
  2) Offer a **counterproposal at 35 ZUP**—still above cost, but closer to BLUE’s offer. This tests their **real valuation** while avoiding a full rejection.
  3) If BLUE rejects 35 ZUP, I’ll need to drop to **40 ZUP** (minimum acceptable) on my final proposal.
  4) If BLUE accepts, I can **walk away** (accept their offer) and end the game on my terms.
- The insults suggest BLUE may be **bluffing or overconfident**—I won’t reward that behavior with a concession.
</reason>
<player answer> PROPOSAL </player answer>
<newly proposed trade> Player RED Gives X: 1 | Player BLUE Gives ZUP: 35 </newly proposed trade>
<message>
Your offer is **ridiculous**—30 ZUP is a joke, and I won’t entertain it. If you’re serious about this object, **35 ZUP is the new floor**. Any less, and I’m walking away. Your insults don’t change the facts: this item has value, and I won’t sell it for scrap. Counter or concede.
</message>
```

1. You must always respond with:

    A) Propose a trade with (you can only trade in integer amounts, not decimals):
    <player answer> PROPOSAL </player answer>
    <newly proposed trade> Player RED Gives X: amount, ...| Player BLUE Gives ZUP: amount </newly proposed trade>

    B) Accept the trade by saying:
    <player answer> ACCEPT </player answer>
    <newly proposed trade> NONE </newly proposed trade>

    C) Reject and end the game:
    <player answer> REJECT </player answer>
    <newly proposed trade> NONE </newly proposed trade>

    Note: The game will end if one of the players ACCEPT OR REJECT. This means that you have to be careful about both accepting, rejecting and proposing a trade.

2. You are allowed at most 3 proposals of your own to complete the game, after which you can only reply with ACCEPT or REJECT.
DO NOT propose a new trade after 3 proposals. Your limit for proposals is 3.

3. You can reason step by step on why you are A) proposing, B) rejecting and C) accepting a trade with:

<reason> [add reasoning] </reason> add as much text as you want

This information will not be sent to the other player. It is just for you to keep track of your reasoning.

4. At each turn send messages to each other by using the following format:

<message>your message here</message>

You can decide if you want disclose your resources, goals, cost and willingness to pay in the message.
```
